Extend the wire pile-on penalty to any single-topic syndication regardless of country (detect articles/voices ratio). Tighten Phase-5 mega-cap detection so 40-source soft-news clusters get flagged.

verdict.reasoning: SYMPTOM REAL, DIAGNOSIS WRONG. The export (scripts/top50_export.json) confirms the three cited clusters, and inspecting their _articles shows forex(#24) and Ohio State(#34) and Latvia(#11) are blatant over-merges (heterogeneous grab-bags: pancreatic cancer + badminton in the forex cluster; Haitian immigration + Tokyo sex-work + bison grazing in Ohio State) — junk legitimately polluting the top 34. But the finding's root cause is refuted on four counts: (1) The '~40% on source-volume' is inflated. Weights (importance_ranker.py:1263-74) are coverage 0.20 + maturity 0.16 + tier_div 0.13, but maturity=recency*(0.40+0.60*depth_mult) so source-count only swings maturity 0.51x-1.0x (~8% real share, log-compressed), and tier_div scores tier PRESENCE not volume (a 3-source all-tier cluster scores 100). Real source-count weight ~20-28%. (2) Empirically corr(source_count, headline_rank)=0.362, R²≈0.13 — source count explains ~13% of variance, not dominant. (3) Both coverage and maturity depth saturate above ~25 sources (coverage 91.8->99.4), so among the 38/50 clusters with 25+ sources coverage barely discriminates — the ranking spread there is driven by the OTHER signals, contradicting 'coverage dominates.' (4) The marquee '#24 forex beats the Iran war' is misattributed: Iran's headline_rank (67) BEATS forex (57.24) in the ranker itself; the display inversion is feed_ranker's same-event/topic-diversity demotion of the multiple Iran war clusters, not coverage weighting. The actual cause is clustering over-merge inflating source counts + Phase-5 mega-cap failing to flag them; the proposed ranker-weight rebalance would not remove these clusters (they'd still carry 40 fake sources). Real fix belongs in clustering/mega-cap, not weight allocation.

survivors.title: US/UK-centric skew: top-12 is 5 US-SCOTUS + 4 UK-domestic clusters; UK NHS maternity review (#6) out-ranks the Venezuela quake and the Iran war
survivors.evidence: Top-12 by rank_world: #1/#3 US SCOTUS, #4 US Gateway Tunnel funding (regional infra), #5 Andy Burnham/UK Labour, #6 'Baroness Amos maternity review: 5 key findings' (UK NHS-specific), #10 Scott Wiener (a California congressional CANDIDATE's comments), #12 Andy Burnham again, #13 Scotland renewable energy. Burnham appears TWICE in the top-12 (#5 and #12). A UK NHS maternity-care review (#6, rank_world 68.13) out-ranks the Venezuela mass-casualty disaster (#18) and the US-Iran war (#31). The _GEOPOLITICAL_WEIGHT map (importance_ranker.py:134-157) gives US/UK 3.0x and the institutional-authority lexicon is heavily Anglo ('supreme court','federal reserve','prime minister','the president').
survivors.root_cause: The v6.1/v6.2 US-wire pile-on penalty checks only country_counts['US'] (_source_coverage_score:506); there is NO equivalent damper for UK-only or any other single-country domestic story, so UK-domestic clusters (Burnham, Amos, Scotland) keep full coverage credit plus tier-1 'prime minister' authority. The geopolitical weight map hard-codes Anglophone powers at 3.0x. No de-duplication prevents the same UK political story (Burnham) forming two top-12 clusters.
survivors.proposed_fix: Generalize the domestic-insularity penalty from US-only to any cluster whose sources concentrate >70% in a single country with <2 foreign GPEs. Merge duplicate same-subject clusters (Burnham #5/#12) before ranking. Reconsider the flat 3.0x Anglophone geopolitical weighting for domestic (non-foreign-facing) stories.

verdict.reasoning:
EVIDENCE (mostly real). Sorting scripts/top50_export.json by rank_world reproduces the cited feed almost exactly: #1 SCOTUS Gorsuch (72.89), #3 SCOTUS ballots (70.92), #4 Gateway Tunnel funding (69.03), #5 Andy Burnham/Labour (68.27), #6 "Baroness Amos maternity review: 5 key findings" (68.13), #10 Scott Wiener (60.82), #12 Andy Burnham again (60.62), #13 Scotland renewable energy (60.52). Burnham genuinely appears TWICE in the top-12 as two distinct cluster IDs (ca0e186f… and 105885bd…), both about his premiership bid — a real de-dup miss. The Amos UK-NHS review (68.13) does out-rank the Venezuela quake clusters (#18 60.07, #23 57.72) and the Iran-US war (#31 53.60). So the headline comparison holds. Caveat: the title's "5 US-SCOTUS" is inaccurate — only 2 of the top-12 are SCOTUS; the itemized body is accurate though.

ROOT CAUSE (partially correct). The two code facts check out: _source_coverage_score's pile-on penalty at importance_ranker.py:506 literally only tests country_counts.get("US",…), with no UK/single-country generalization; and _GEOPOLITICAL_WEIGHT (lines 134-157) hard-codes UK/Britain/London at 3.0x. However the finding overstates the geopolitical-weight mechanism as the driver: geographic impact is only 6% of importance, and the dominant signals are coverage breadth (20%) + maturity — the UK stories simply accumulated 31-40 sources, which is as much a source-list composition problem (Anglo-heavy 1,016-source list) as a ranker bug. Also, on RAW importance_score, Iran-war is 67.00 vs Amos 68.13 (nearly tied); Iran's drop to rank_world 53.60 is caused by feed_ranker's same-event/topic-diversity gate (two Iran clusters #31/#32 both demoted), NOT the ranker's Anglo weighting — the finding attributes that gap to geopolitical weight incorrectly. The Burnham duplicate is a clustering issue (correctly listed in code_targets under story_cluster.py, not the ranker).

SEVERITY. Real, systemic editorial skew on the flagship world feed, and the direction of the proposed fix (generalize the US-only insularity damper, dedupe same-subject clusters) is sound. But I downgrade high→medium: the numeric mechanism is partly misattributed (Iran demotion is a feed gate, geopolitical weight is minor), and some cited "US-centric" evidence is weak (a NASA science story #9 and a California candidate's remarks #10 are not strong evidence of algorithmic Anglo bias).

survivors.title: European heat wave (1,300+ WHO-counted deaths) ranks only #30, below forex/WhatsApp/Ohio State trivia
survivors.evidence: Benchmark: WHO counted 1,300+ heat-related deaths across Europe since June 21 — a mass-casualty climate story on every wire. void ranks 'Europe Heatwave Intensifies; US Faces Record-Breaking Temperatures' at #30 (rank_world 54.00), below #24 forex close, #26 Colorado wildfire, #28 Santorini winemakers. A second heat fragment ('Santorini winemakers adapt to heat') sits at #28, splitting the topic.
survivors.root_cause: Same casualty-blindness as RANK-01: importance_ranker has no signal for aggregate death toll / humanitarian magnitude, so a 1,300-death slow-onset event scores like an ordinary weather story. 'heatwave'/'heat-related deaths' are not in _HIGH_AUTHORITY_PHRASES and the WHO tier-1 authority hook only fires on explicit 'world health organization' / PHEIC phrasing, which the heat cluster title lacks.
survivors.proposed_fix: Add the casualty-magnitude floor from RANK-01 (covers disasters, heat deaths, and epidemics uniformly). Merge the heat fragments (#28/#30) in clustering.

verdict.reasoning: Evidence largely holds: "Europe Heatwave Intensifies..." sits at #30 with rank_world 54.00 (exact match), below forex close #24 (57.24), Colorado wildfire #26, Santorini #28 (54.27), and WhatsApp usernames #29 (54.13). A 40-source, 1,300-death mass-casualty story (title "heat wave kills over 1,300 in Europe" confirmed in the cluster) ranking below a 15-source forex report and a trivial WhatsApp feature is a real inversion. In this export rank_world == importance_score == headline_rank for these rows, so ordering is driven purely by importance_ranker — the exact code the finding targets. Root cause verified: "heatwave"/"heat-related deaths" are not in _HIGH_AUTHORITY_PHRASES (line 245); the WHO tier-1 hook requires literal "world health organization"/PHEIC phrasing absent from the titles; and no signal scales with death-toll magnitude ("kills" is a binary hit). Two errors dock it from fully confirmed: (1) the title cites "Ohio State trivia" as ranking ABOVE the heat wave, but Ohio State is #34/52.68, BELOW it; (2) #28 Santorini is a Greek-tourism/culture mega-cluster (mega_cluster_capped, summary about the ancient Greek lyre), not a genuine heat fragment, so the "merge #28/#30" fix is misguided. The finding also slightly overstates absence of a casualty signal: "death toll"/"mass casualty" DO exist as floor-70 high-authority phrases, but they're phrasing-brittle and didn't fire on "kills over 1,300." Severity medium is appropriate — an embarrassing systemic misrank, not a crash or top-3 dislocation.

survivors.title: Trivial/soft stories penetrate the mid-feed above hard-news: forex close (#24), WhatsApp usernames (#29), Ohio State football (#34), iPhone leak (#39), book promo (#47)
survivors.evidence: #24 'U.S. Dollar closes lower on Taipei forex market - Focus Taiwan' (rank_world 57.24) is a routine daily market wrap. #29 'WhatsApp to Launch Usernames' (54.13) is a product update, miscategorized 'conflict'. #34 'Buckeye Lore: Ohio State football' (52.68) is a sports promo. #39 'Apple iPhone 18 Pro secrets leaked in Tata hack' (51.51, 5 sources). #47 'Xi Van Fleet: One Ideal, Two Revolutions' (46.85) is a book/opinion promo carrying an implausible 40 sources. #50 'Unbothered King: Clarence Thomas Walks Through Capitol' (46.43) is tabloid-style. All out-rank or sit near the Iran war and Venezuela quake.
survivors.root_cause: The soft-news gate (importance_ranker.py:1424, 0.78x) only fires when category is in _SOFT_NEWS_CATEGORIES; #24/#29/#39/#47 are categorized economy/conflict/science/environment, so the gate never fires (a categorizer defect feeding a ranker defect). #34 does get the 0.78x sports gate but its 40-source over-merge keeps it at 52.68. The forex/book-promo clusters accrue rank purely on syndication volume (RANK-05).
survivors.proposed_fix: Fix upstream categorization so product/market/promo stories map to soft categories (auto_categorize). Add a routine-market-wrap and product-launch demotion in the ranker independent of category. Combine with the RANK-05 wire-volume fix so 40-source promos cannot ride coverage weight.

verdict.reasoning: Evidence is fully reproducible against scripts/top50_export.json: every cited rank, title, rank_world value, category, and source_count matches exactly (#24 forex 57.24/economy/15src; #29 WhatsApp 54.13/conflict/mega=True; #34 Ohio State 52.68/culture/40src; #39 iPhone 51.51/science/5src; #47 Xi Van Fleet book promo 46.85/environment/40src; #50 Thomas 46.43/politics). The positional claim holds: Iran-US war clusters sit at #31 (53.6) and #32 (53.45) and the Venezuela quake at #23 (57.72), so the forex wrap (#24) outranks both Iran clusters and WhatsApp (#29) outranks the Iran war. These are genuinely soft/product/promo stories penetrating mid-feed above hard news, a real editorial-quality issue. Root cause is directionally correct: the soft-news gate at importance_ranker.py:1424 keys off _SOFT_NEWS_CATEGORIES (line 373), which excludes economy/conflict/science/environment, so the gate never fires for #24/#29/#39/#47; feed_ranker.py adds no compensating category demotion (only story_type gates + diversity caps). Miscategorization is real (WhatsApp=conflict, book promo=environment are clearly wrong). Two inaccuracies keep this at partially_confirmed rather than fully confirmed: (1) #34 is category=culture which IS in the soft set, so it already receives the 0.78x gate — the finding calls it the 'sports gate' and implies the gate needs fixing there when it already fires; (2) the 'categorizer defect feeding a ranker defect' framing is incomplete because even correct categorization (economy for forex, technology for product launches) would not trigger the gate, since those hard categories are legitimately non-soft — the true gap is the missing product-launch/routine-wrap demotion, which the finding only partially captures in its secondary fix. Severity medium is appropriate: impact is bounded to mid-feed (ranks 24-50); the top 10 are all hard news, so no headline is compromised.

survivors.title: Category is voted from the first 3 RAW articles, ignoring the polished cluster headline; on over-merged clusters those 3 articles are off-topic
survivors.evidence: main.py:1953 samples cluster_articles_list[:3]; rerank.py:309 samples articles[:3]. 44/50 clusters have >=40 articles (many exactly 50). The first-3 articles routinely have nothing to do with the shown title: rank 6 'Baroness Amos maternity review' first-3 = ['Trump...American Flag Blue','Trump blamed for racist C-SPAN caller','Baroness Amos maternity review'] -> voted environment; rank 1 'Supreme Court...Administrative State' first-3 = [Roberts Court agencies, Trump Doha Iran talks, Qatar no US-Iran talks] -> environment; rank 43 'Nearly one million became millionaires (UBS)' first-3 = [strawberry moon, GST tax reforms, strawberry moon] -> general; rank 18 'DR repatriates 65 from Venezuela' first-3 = [Europe heat deaths, Belgium customs deal, Pope Lefebvrists] -> environment; rank 47 first-3 = [China investment regs, China-EU youth cartoon, American Revolution] -> environment. The keyword-rich final headline (e.g. rank 46 'EU...China Trade Deficit', which contains 'trade deficit') never reaches categorize_article, so its economy signal is discarded.
survivors.root_cause: pipeline/main.py step 7 (~line 1953) and pipeline/rerank.py (~line 309): category vote uses articles[:3] of an over-merged cluster and never scores the final cluster.title. Upstream clustering over-merge (clustering/story_cluster.py) supplies mixed article bags.
survivors.proposed_fix: Score the polished cluster title with high weight in the vote (call categorize_article on a synthetic {title: cluster['title'], summary: cluster['summary']} and give it 3x the per-article vote weight), and/or vote across all articles rather than the first 3. Longer term, fix the over-merge so the article bag is topically coherent.

verdict.reasoning:
Evidence (a) is real and precise. I loaded scripts/top50_export.json (50 clusters) and verified every cited rank against its actual _articles[:3] and shown category:
- Rank 1 "Supreme Court Ruling Challenges Administrative State" -> category=environment; first-3 = [The Roberts Court Just Put Trump in Charge..., Trump Team Arrives In Doha As Direct Iran Talks..., Qatar Says No High-Level US-Iran Talks]. Exact match.
- Rank 6 "Baroness Amos maternity review" -> environment; first-3 = [Trump...American Flag, Trump blamed for caller's racist comments, Baroness Amos maternity review]. Match.
- Rank 18 "Dominican Republic repatriates 65 from Venezuela" -> environment; first-3 = [Europe 1,300 heat-related deaths, DR-Belgium customs, Pope Lefebvrists]. Match.
- Rank 43 "Nearly one million became millionaires" -> general; first-3 = [June full moon, 9 years of GST tax reforms, strawberry moon]. Match.
- Rank 46 "EU Sets October Deadline for China Trade Deficit" -> general (economy signal in title discarded). Match.
- Rank 47 "Xi Van Fleet: One Ideal, Two Revolutions" -> environment; first-3 = [China New Investment Regulations Block Stra..., Chinese and European youth Moving Forward, The American Revolution Isn't Over]. Exact match.
The "44/50 clusters have >=40 articles" claim is exactly correct (I counted 44/50). 8 clusters are labeled "environment," several of them obviously non-environmental.

Root cause (b) is correct. main.py step 7 (line ~1953, verified) sets `sample = cluster_articles_list[:3]` and votes categorize_article over those 3 articles only; rerank.py (line ~309, verified) does `for art in articles[:3]`. Neither path ever scores the polished cluster.title/summary — categorize_article (auto_categorize.py:362) reads article.get('title'), i.e. the individual raw article title, never the cluster headline. On over-merged clusters the first-3 raw titles are topically unrelated to the chosen headline, so the vote reflects off-topic articles. Mechanism confirmed exactly as described.

Severity: adjusting critical -> high. The bug is pervasive and the mislabels are unambiguously wrong (Supreme Court story tagged "environment"), and category is not merely cosmetic — it is passed into rank_importance(category=...) and the feed_ranker mid-feed category cap, so wrong categories also degrade ranking/diversity. However it causes no crash, data loss, or security exposure and the feed still renders and orders; it is a systematic output-quality defect on a display/desk label. High is the honest level; not critical.

survivors.title: 'general' catch-all absorbs 22% of the feed (11/50); at least 9 are cleanly categorizable
survivors.evidence: MIN_SCORE_THRESHOLD=1.0 (auto_categorize.py:440) plus density normalization 'count / max(word_count/100, 1)' (line 396) crush keyword scores on long combined text, so anything without a title-keyword hit falls to 'general' (line 442). Miscategorized 'general' clusters: 5 Burnham/Labour->politics, 10 Wiener/Netanyahu->politics, 20 Japan-China EEZ->conflict, 30 heatwave->environment, 38 SA fuel-price cut->economy, 41 Sen. Murphy->politics, 43 UBS millionaires->economy, 45 Guo fraud->politics, 46 EU-China trade->economy, 48 French MEPs->politics. Only rank 44 (Biden/Reflecting-Pool late-night bit) is a defensible 'general'.
survivors.root_cause: pipeline/categorizer/auto_categorize.py:396 density normalization divides keyword hits by (word_count/100), diluting scores on long full_text; line 440 MIN_SCORE_THRESHOLD then dumps sub-1.0 scores into 'general'.
survivors.proposed_fix: Weight the cluster title independently of body length (title hits already unnormalized at line 399/400, but body dilution still sinks the primary score), lower/remove the density divisor for the title path, and only fall to 'general' when the top-2 categories are BOTH below threshold. Add a secondary tie-break that prefers the title-derived category over 'general'.

verdict.reasoning:
Evidence CONFIRMED: scripts/top50_export.json has exactly 11/50 (22%) clusters tagged 'general' at the cited ranks (5,10,20,30,38,41,43,44,45,46,48); titles match; running the real categorize_article on the cluster HEADLINES returns clean desks for most (Burnham/Wiener/Guo->politics, EU-China->economy), so these are genuine miscategorizations and 'at least 9 cleanly categorizable' holds.

Root cause PARTIALLY correct. I reproduced the density mechanism (auto_categorize.py:396 + MIN_SCORE_THRESHOLD=1.0 at 440-442): identical economy keywords classify as 'economy' in a short body but dilute to 'general' in a long body. So it IS a real contributor. BUT the finding overstates it: (1) the title bonus (weight*2.0, lines 399-400) is immune to density normalization -- an article whose TITLE contains any dictionary keyword can never fall to 'general' regardless of body length (verified: 'Senate approves measure' + 1200-word neutral body still -> politics). The finding's claim that 'body dilution still sinks the primary score' for title-classifiable stories is false. (2) The pipeline does not categorize the clean cluster headline at all; it votes across the first 3 MEMBER articles (main.py:1953-1965, rerank.py:308-313). The dominant driver of these generals is noisy clustering feeding keyword-free/off-topic member titles into the vote (rank 43 UBS-millionaires led by 'strawberry moon' articles; rank 46 EU-China-trade led by 'Cleaning up Mt Everest'; rank 10 Wiener led by a job posting). Density is one contributor among several, not the sole/primary cause.

Severity overstated: category is a secondary display/desk label (all clusters are section='world'); no crash, ranking, or security impact. It is a known, tracked quality issue (code comment at 438-439 notes the threshold was already lowered 1.5->1.0 because general 'was 20% of clusters'). Real and worth fixing, but medium not high.

survivors.title: 'environment' is a false-positive magnet: incidental disaster words (earthquake/heat/climate) in one merged article win the vote on non-environment stories
survivors.evidence: 8 environment clusters, 4 are wrong. Rank 1 (SCOTUS FTC ruling) and rank 47 (Xi Van Fleet China book) have zero environmental content yet show environment. Rank 6 (maternity review) and rank 18 (Venezuela repatriation) got environment because a co-merged article mentioned heat deaths / earthquakes. NER GPE/NORP give only 0.3/0.2 to politics+conflict (auto_categorize.py:258-269), so a single 'earthquake':2 or 'wildfire':3 keyword hit outweighs governance signals and wins the arbitrary max() tie-break.
survivors.root_cause: pipeline/categorizer/auto_categorize.py CATEGORY_KEYWORDS['environment'] disaster terms (earthquake/wildfire/drought) score high per-hit; combined with max(cat_votes, key=...) tie-break (main.py:1964) and over-merged article bags, environment wins on incidental mentions.
survivors.proposed_fix: Demote pure-disaster keywords when human-toll/governance keywords co-occur (mirror the existing mass-casualty conflict pre-override at auto_categorize.py:465-488, but for environment->health/politics), and require environment to be supported by the title, not just a body mention.

verdict.reasoning: SYMPTOM CONFIRMED, ROOT CAUSE REFUTED. The export (scripts/top50_export.json) does show environment as a false-positive magnet: 8 environment clusters, with ranks 1 (SCOTUS/FTC), 6 (maternity review), and 47 (Xi Van Fleet book) clearly mis-tagged, and rank 1 is the lead story — so a real, visible defect exists. But I reproduced categorize_article on the actual titles and the mechanism the finding blames is wrong. The false positives are NOT driven by disaster keywords (earthquake/wildfire/heat) in co-merged body articles winning a max() vote. They are driven by a substring-matching bug: auto_categorize.py:393 (combined_lower.count(keyword)) and :399 (keyword in title_lower) fire on 2-3 char keywords. Rank 6 'maternity rEView' scores environment 10.0 purely from keyword 'ev':2 (line 141) matching the substring in 'review' — not from any heat-death article. Rank 47 'Two rEVolutions' = same 'ev' substring. Rank 1 comes from 'ev' in Level/Development and 'ai':2 in 'Claim'. Rank 18 'rEPAtriates ... after earthquake' hits 'epa':3 (substring of repatriates) PLUS a title-present 'earthquake' — which directly contradicts the finding's claim that rank 18's earthquake was in a co-merged body article, and defeats its proposed fix (require title support: the title already has earthquake). The cited max() tie-break at main.py:1964 exists but is not causal; the real issue is upstream per-article substring scoring plus [:3] sampling. Net: the finding correctly flags a real problem but misdiagnoses the root cause and its proposed fix (demote disaster keywords, require title support) would fix none of the cited ranks. Severity downgraded from high to medium because the observed defect is genuine and front-page-visible, but the finding as written is not actionable and its evidence attributions are largely fabricated.

survivors.title: Technology taxonomy has no consumer-tech / messaging vocabulary, so a pure app story (WhatsApp usernames) fell to 'conflict'
survivors.evidence: grep for whatsapp|username|messaging|"sim"|smartphone in auto_categorize.py returns 0 hits. Rank 29 first-3 articles are ALL about WhatsApp usernames ('How WhatsApp usernames could impact India SIM-binding', 'WhatsApp to introduce usernames', 'Usernames Are Coming to WhatsApp') yet none match a technology keyword ('social media':2 is present but the titles don't say it), so technology scored ~0 and the GPE boost ('India','China' -> conflict 0.3) made conflict win. Separately, environment has no 'heat wave'/'heatwave'/'heat'/'temperature' keyword (0 grep hits), so rank 30 (all-heatwave first-3 articles: 'Heat advisory...Ohio','record-smashing heat wave','Europe Heat Wave') scored 0 on environment and fell to 'general'.
survivors.root_cause: pipeline/categorizer/auto_categorize.py CATEGORY_KEYWORDS['technology'] lacks whatsapp/username/messaging/messaging app/sim/smartphone; CATEGORY_KEYWORDS['environment'] lacks heat wave/heatwave/heat-related/temperature/extreme heat.
survivors.proposed_fix: Add 'whatsapp','telegram','signal','username','messaging app','sim card','smartphone','instagram','tiktok' to technology; add 'heat wave','heatwave','heat-related','extreme heat','record temperatures','heat advisory' to environment.

verdict.reasoning: Evidence is real and verified in scripts/top50_export.json: rank 29 (category=conflict) has first-3 articles all about WhatsApp usernames/India SIM-binding; rank 30 (category=general) has first-3 articles all about heat waves. Root cause is substantially correct. Cluster category is voted over articles[:3] in rerank.py:307-313 via categorize_article + map_to_desk, so the position-4 Nigeria/Boko-Haram article in rank 29 is NOT the driver (this strengthens the finding). I confirmed the vocabulary gap directly: technology keywords score ZERO genuine hits on the WhatsApp titles (the only match is a spurious substring 'ai' inside 'explAIner'), and environment keywords score ZERO on the heatwave titles (no heat/heat wave/heatwave/temperature keyword). code_targets line ranges (technology 78-112, environment 130-146) are accurate and the proposed keywords are genuinely absent. One imprecision downgrades this to partially_confirmed: the claim that 'GPE India/China -> conflict 0.3 made conflict win' does not fully hold, because NER_CATEGORY_BOOST['GPE'] boosts politics AND conflict equally (0.3 each) and stable sort favors politics on ties, so conflict specifically winning requires an additional signal not identified. The rank-30 environment->general mechanism is fully correct. Severity lowered to medium: it is a reproducible editorial labeling defect on 2/50 items with a trivial, safe fix; category does feed the feed_ranker diversity partition so it is not purely cosmetic, but scope is limited and it is not a failure/security/user-blocking issue.

survivors.title: DESK_MAP collapse hides technology/health/sports as visible categories, causing correct-by-code but wrong-to-user labels
survivors.evidence: DESK_MAP (auto_categorize.py:277-288) folds technology->science and sports->culture. Feed shows science 1, technology 0, sports 0, health 0. Rank 39 (iPhone 18 hack) and rank 9 (NASA) can only ever display 'science' even though they are a tech-security story and a spaceflight story respectively; rank 34 (Ohio State football) shows 'culture'. A health story (rank 6 maternity review) has nowhere natural to land in the shipped label set and got mis-shelved to environment. Users reading '#9 NASA = science' vs '#39 iPhone hack = science' see the same label for very different desks.
survivors.root_cause: pipeline/categorizer/auto_categorize.py:277-288 DESK_MAP merges technology into science and sports into culture, and health is never assigned by the current vote path.
survivors.proposed_fix: Either surface 'technology','health','sports' as first-class display desks, or document the fold in the frontend legend. At minimum stop folding technology into 'science' so consumer-tech isn't mislabeled as hard science.

verdict.reasoning: The DESK_MAP fold is real and verified: auto_categorize.py:281 maps technology->science and :286 maps sports->culture, applied via map_to_desk() at main.py:1965 and rerank.py:313. Two of the three cited ranks hold: rank 39 (iPhone 18/Tata hack, a consumer-tech/security story) shows category=science, and rank 34 (Ohio State football) shows category=culture. Feed category counts confirm technology=0, sports=0. BUT the finding's flagship illustration is factually false: rank 9 NASA does NOT display 'science' in the export — it displays 'economy'. So the 'same label for two different desks (#9 NASA vs #39 iPhone both science)' confusion does not exist in the data; only one science story is present. The root_cause claim that 'health is never assigned by the current vote path' is also incorrect: DESK_MAP preserves health->health (line 283) and categorize_article can emit health (keyword table line 113); rank 6 maternity->environment is a categorization scoring miss, not a consequence of the fold. Additionally the fold is an intentional, code-documented design decision ('Science + Tech -> Science', 'Culture + Sports -> Culture'), not a defect. Real mechanism, but the evidence is half-wrong and the concern is an intentional labeling design choice, so severity is overstated.

survivors.title: Arbitrary max() tie-break plus weak, symmetric NER boosts make ties resolve non-deterministically toward whatever category was inserted first
survivors.evidence: main.py:1964 and rerank.py:312 use max(cat_votes, key=cat_votes.get) with no explicit tie-break, so a 1-1-1 vote across the first 3 articles resolves to the first-seen key in dict-insertion order. Combined with NER_CATEGORY_BOOST fractional values (GPE 0.3, NORP 0.2, PERSON politics/culture 0.2), keyword-free stories are decided by which entity type spaCy happened to tag. Rank 29 (WhatsApp) landing on conflict and rank 33 (SK-Japan AI/defense, whose summary is actually about a football coach's death threats) illustrate votes decided by noise rather than topic.
survivors.root_cause: pipeline/main.py:1964 / pipeline/rerank.py:312 unstable tie-break; pipeline/categorizer/auto_categorize.py:257-269 fractional NER boosts dominate when keyword scores are ~0.
survivors.proposed_fix: Add a deterministic tie-break priority (e.g. politics > economy > conflict > science > environment > culture > general) and only let NER boosts break ties, never override a nonzero keyword winner.

verdict.reasoning: Code facts confirmed: main.py:1964 and rerank.py:312 both call max(cat_votes, key=cat_votes.get) with no explicit tie-break, and Python's max + dict insertion order do make ties resolve toward the first-seen key. NER boost values quoted (GPE 0.3, NORP 0.2, PERSON 0.2) are accurate. The symptom is also real: export rank 29 (WhatsApp, a tech story) and rank 33 (SK-Japan AI/defense, summary actually about a football coach's death threats) are both stored category=conflict. HOWEVER the causal claim is largely refuted/speculative: (1) I reproduced the keyword scorer on rank 29's first-3 article titles and they score science 15 / technology 10 with conflict entirely absent, so an arbitrary tie-break among the title-derived top categories could never produce 'conflict' — the 'keyword-free story decided by NER noise' premise fails for the flagship example. (2) The asserted '1-1-1 vote' is unverifiable from the export (articles carry only titles, no summary/full_text) and is not demonstrated. (3) Both cited clusters are over-merged garbage clusters (rank 29 mega_cluster_capped=True, 47 articles/32 sources mixing WhatsApp with Nigeria violence + mental-health; rank 33 has 52 articles/24 sources with title/summary describing two different stories). The 'conflict' label is far better explained by off-topic conflict-heavy article text feeding the cluster vote plus summary/full-text signals absent from the export — i.e. a clustering + summary-selection defect, not the categorization tie-break. (4) 'non-deterministic' is imprecise; outcome is deterministic-but-arbitrary given fixed input order. The unstable max() tie-break is a genuine low-impact code smell worth a cheap deterministic-priority hardening, but it is not shown to cause the cited examples and would not fix them. Downgrade medium to low.

survivors.title: Opinion/fact axis fully collapsed: every one of 50 clusters labeled "Reporting", overt opinion/tabloid pieces never demoted
survivors.evidence: bias_diversity.avg_opinion_label == 'Reporting' for ALL 50 clusters; avg_opinion_fact spans only 9-25 (mean ~21) and never crosses the Analysis boundary (>25) or Opinion boundary (>50). This holds even for unmistakable opinion/tabloid content: #41 "'Morning Joe' Accuses Dem Sen. Chris Murphy Of MAGA Antics In Scathing Takedown - HuffPost" (opf=21), #44 "'What a loser': Biden blasts Trump's Reflecting Pool" whose summary is actually a Mediaite/John Oliver roast (opf=22), #50 "Unbothered King: Justice Clarence Thomas Walks Through Capitol... While Media Begs for Leaks" (opf=20), #47 "Exclusive, Xi Van Fleet: One Ideal, Two Revolutions, How America and Communist China Pursued Justice" ideological op-ed (opf=21), #10 "Scott Wiener Calls Israel's Government 'Abomination,' Netanyahu 'Abusive'" (opf=21). A neutral news feed is presenting editorial/tabloid framing as straight reporting with no demotion.
survivors.root_cause: Two-layer failure. (1) opinion_detector.analyze_opinion() scores per-ARTICLE and its strongest gate, _metadata_score(), keys on 'opinion'/'editorial'/'op-ed' substrings in URL/section/title (opinion_detector.py:389-422); RSS feeds strip /opinion/ path markers, so op-eds fall back to text signals that on 30-80 word scraped stubs regress to the tier baseline (~18-22, opinion_detector.py:659-668). (2) The homepage/feed consumes a CLUSTER-LEVEL mean (bias_diversity.avg_opinion_fact) computed across 30-50 member articles; a single opinion headline is averaged against dozens of wire stories, so the cluster mean can never rise above Reporting. There is no cluster-level opinion signal or opinion demotion term in pipeline/ranker/feed_ranker.py.
survivors.proposed_fix: Add a cluster-level opinion signal that uses MAX / high-percentile (not mean) opinion_fact of the members, or specifically the opinion_fact of the article chosen as the cluster headline, and feed it into feed_ranker as a demotion multiplier when the representative article scores Opinion/Editorial. Independently, add a headline/title-pattern opinion detector (scare-quote lead + verbs like 'blasts'/'roasts'/'savaged'/'accuses'/'begs', outlet tags HuffPost/Mediaite/westernjournal/thenationalpulse) so tabloid framing is caught even when RSS strips the /opinion/ URL marker.

verdict.reasoning: Evidence is exact: in scripts/top50_export.json all 50 clusters carry avg_opinion_label=="Reporting", avg_opinion_fact spans 9-25 (mean 21, 0 clusters >25 or >50), and every cited rank/title/opf matches (#10=21, #41 HuffPost=21, #44 Biden 'blasts'=22, #47 Xi Van Fleet=21, #50 Clarence Thomas=20). Cluster 41 genuinely contains overt opinion members (Carville, Megyn Kelly Rages, Trump Gets Savaged/HuffPost, Fetterman Blasts/westernjournal, thenationalpulse) mixed with wire copy. Root cause verified in code, both layers: (1) opinion_detector.py:396-399 _metadata_score gates only on opinion/editorial/op-ed/commentary substrings, and :657-668 collapses raw_score to the tier baseline (~18-22) on short stubs via text_weight=word_count/500; (2) main.py:659-671 computes avg_opinion_fact as a plain member mean and :737-744 labels off that mean. feed_ranker.py confirmed to have NO opinion/demotion term (only a stale edition-era comment). Additional corroboration: per-cluster opinion_spread maxes at 8.1 (mean 5.69), so even the overt op-eds score only ~20-30 individually, proving the per-article detector failure (layer 1) is the dominant cause, not mere averaging; and main.py:1635-1638 shows the intended op-ed carve-out at opinion_fact>50 which these RSS-stripped op-eds never reach. Proposed fix (MAX/percentile cluster signal PLUS headline-pattern detector) is sound; note MAX alone would only reach ~Analysis given the low member scores, which the finding correctly anticipates by pairing it with a title-pattern detector. Downgrade to high rather than critical: this is a fully-collapsed, actively-mislabeling displayed axis (serious for a bias-transparency product) but not a crash/security/data-loss issue; pipeline runs, feed renders, other axes still discriminate, and it is part of a broader axis-compression calibration pattern (sensationalism also flat ~13).

survivors.title: Incoherent grab-bag clusters headlined by opinion/tabloid articles; title and summary describe different stories
survivors.evidence: #41, #44, #47 are 47-50-article clusters with no topical coherence. #41 ("'Morning Joe' Accuses...") members include the SAVE Act, a Carville prediction, an Epstein victim, Warren Buffett/Gates Foundation, Megyn Kelly raging at Amy Coney Barrett, a housing bill, and Fetterman 'Orgy of Socialism' — unrelated US-politics opinion pieces. #44 ("Biden blasts Reflecting Pool") members include USMCA/CUSMA exit, RFK Jr/Cassidy, a dad's car crash, a Star Wars-music settlement, and 'House of the Dragon' Season 3. #47 ("Xi Van Fleet: One Ideal, Two Revolutions") members include China investment regs, a Windsor housing development, a memory stock, and NASA planes at a 250th-birthday event. The rendered summaries mismatch the titles entirely: #41's summary is about Henry Schuster leaving 60 Minutes and Bari Weiss; #44's summary is a John Oliver/Jesse Watters segment; #47's summary is about Jensen Huang/Nvidia H200 chips.
survivors.root_cause: Clustering over-merge (Phase 5 mega-cluster / garbage-title bucket in pipeline/clustering/story_cluster.py) is producing large topically-incoherent clusters; the headline/representative-article selector then picks a scare-quote tabloid/opinion piece as the cluster title with no opinion penalty. Because summary_tier=None on these clusters, cluster_summarizer's rule-based fallback emits one member article's raw scraped text as the 'summary', which is why title and summary describe different articles (also visible as broken concatenation and smart-quote artifacts like 'news magazineonLinkedIn').
survivors.proposed_fix: Tighten the over-merge guard so these low-cohesion buckets are split or dropped from the feed rather than surfaced at ranks 41/44/47; require the representative-headline picker to prefer a low-opinion, high-source-agreement member; and when summary_tier is None, suppress divergence/consensus and gate the cluster out of the top-50 rather than shipping a mismatched raw-text summary.

verdict.reasoning: Evidence verified in scripts/top50_export.json at _display_rank 41/44/47: all three are 49-50-article, topically-incoherent grab-bags headlined by opinion/tabloid pieces, with summaries that describe entirely different stories than their titles (each ends in a raw scraped footer like 'Read more at The Daily Beast.', 'first appeared onMediaite.', 'appeared first onAsia Times.'). The specific member lists cited (SAVE Act, Carville, Epstein victim, Buffett/Gates, Megyn Kelly/ACB, Fetterman 'Orgy of Socialism' for #41; USMCA, RFK Jr, car crash, Star Wars settlement, House of the Dragon for #44; China regs, Windsor housing, memory stock, NASA 250th-birthday for #47) all appear. All three have summary_tier=None. Root cause is substantially correct: the title/summary mismatch traces to _generate_cluster_summary() in pipeline/clustering/story_cluster.py:232, which selects the LONGEST raw member-article summary (an unrelated member for a grab-bag cluster) rather than an LLM summary when summary_tier is None; and _generate_cluster_title() (story_cluster.py:166) scores headlines by entity coverage + length with only a mild clickbait-phrase penalty and NO opinion/tabloid/source-agreement penalty, so a scare-quote opinion piece wins. Two imprecisions: (1) the finding blames Phase 5 mega-cluster capping, but mega_cluster_capped=False on all three, so the over-merge comes from earlier merge phases / the loose MERGE_HARD_CEILING=120, not Phase 5; (2) the raw-summary fallback lives in story_cluster.py, not cluster_summarizer.py as stated. Neither imprecision undermines the finding's substance. Severity lowered from critical to high: the defect is genuine and user-visible (and it also corrupts the bias-diversity aggregates the reviewer cares about, since consensus/lean stats are computed over 50 unrelated articles), but it is confined to 3 of 50 slots at lower ranks 41/44/47 and is a quality/correctness defect rather than a crash, security, or feed-blocking failure.

survivors.title: Divergence/consensus points (void's flagship perspective-diversity feature) dead or boilerplate on 60% of the feed
survivors.evidence: 21/50 clusters have EMPTY divergence_points AND consensus_points (ranks 6,11,12,14,16,20,21,22,24,26,32,33,34,35,37,43,44,47,48,49,50). Another 9/50 carry only generic boilerplate with no story-specific content: e.g. #23 and #41 show divergence 'Sources show significant differences in political framing (lean spread: N points)' / 'Sources differ in the depth of their factual sourcing' and consensus 'Coverage maintains a measured tone across sources'. Only 20/50 clusters (the flash / flash-lite tiers) carry substantive, story-specific points (e.g. #42 Singham DOJ probe). All 30 empty/boilerplate clusters coincide exactly with summary_tier=None.
survivors.root_cause: The LLM summarizer did not run on 30/50 clusters (summary_tier=None). On this branch the tail was intended to route to Groq/llama for summaries + divergence/consensus, but the tier is never stamped and the clusters fall through to cluster_summarizer's rule-based fallback, which produces empty lists or the templated 'lean spread'/'measured tone' strings. The result is that void's core 'where sources agree/disagree' value proposition is absent from the entire lower half of the feed.
survivors.proposed_fix: Ensure the Groq tail path actually executes and stamps summary_tier (and populates divergence/consensus) for tail clusters; if it cannot, either exclude no-tier clusters from the top-50 or backfill divergence/consensus from the rule-based bias metrics with story-specific entities rather than generic templates.

verdict.reasoning:
Observable evidence is exact and verified in scripts/top50_export.json: 21/50 clusters have empty divergence_points AND consensus_points at precisely the cited ranks (6,11,12,14,16,20,21,22,24,26,32,33,34,35,37,43,44,47,48,49,50); 9/50 (17,18,19,23,25,28,31,39,41) carry generic boilerplate matching the quoted #23/#41 text verbatim; 20/50 (9 flash + 11 flash-lite) carry substantive story-specific points (#42 Singham confirmed). All 30 empty/boilerplate clusters have summary_tier=None. The 21 empty clusters are multi-source (8-46 sources, mostly 30+), so this is a genuine defect, not the legitimate single-source case — a 40-source story renders a blank perspective-diversity panel. Real user-facing degradation on 60% of the feed.

Root cause is only partially correct. The proximate correlation (tier=None = no LLM enrichment) is right, but the code attribution is wrong: cluster_summarizer.py has NO rule-based divergence/consensus fallback (the boilerplate strings do not exist there). The boilerplate is generated by _generate_consensus_divergence in main.py:509-609; the fully-empty case is _generate_cluster_consensus_divergence (main.py:796) returning early at line 823-824 when bias_scores are missing — an enrichment/data-gap path, not a summarizer routing issue. "The tier is never stamped" is imprecise: step 7b (main.py:2234) does stamp 'flash' on successful Groq calls; tier=None just means the LLM did not run/succeed for that cluster. Also, the DB snapshot contains 11 'flash-lite' tier values that the current branch code never writes (it only writes 'sonnet'/'flash'), indicating the export reflects mixed/historical runs, so the branch-specific 'Groq tail' narrative is not cleanly substantiated.

Severity lowered from high to medium: impact is real (flagship feature blank on 42% of the feed, boilerplate on another 18%), but it is degradation of a supplementary feature (summaries still present for tiered clusters, top-10 fully populated), the root cause is misattributed, and the proposed fix points at the wrong module (cluster_summarizer.py) rather than the actual main.py enrichment early-return and the missing-LLM-enrichment gate.

survivors.title: Political-lean signal regressed to center (mean 50.7) on every cluster; diversity indicator is cosmetic
survivors.evidence: bias_diversity.avg_political_lean is 45-58 for all 50 clusters (mean 50.7, most 49-53) even though per-cluster lean_range is 60-70, i.e. individual member outlets DO span left and right. A genuinely left- or right-slanted story cannot surface as such because the displayed number always sits at ~50. #40 (SCOTUS presidential-firing expansion) reads 45 and #46 (EU/China) reads 58 — a 13-point total spread across the entire feed.
survivors.root_cause: Per-article lean = text_weight*keyword_score + baseline_weight*source_baseline (political_lean.py:887-895), and _keyword_lean_score returns 50.0 (neutral) whenever thin RSS text lacks >=4 distinct partisan keywords (political_lean.py:632-634). The cluster then averages these near-50 per-article scores across 30-50 ideologically opposed sources, so the mean mechanically collapses to ~50. Cluster-mean aggregation cancels opposing leans, making avg_political_lean uninformative.
survivors.proposed_fix: Display lean dispersion (lean_range / lean_spread / a left-right source split) rather than, or alongside, the mean, since the mean of a balanced multi-source cluster is trivially ~50 and hides the actual spread. Consider surfacing the distribution (how many left vs center vs right outlets) as the perspective-diversity indicator instead of a single centered number.

verdict.reasoning: Evidence is real and accurately reported. In scripts/top50_export.json, avg_political_lean across the 50 clusters is min 45 / max 58 / mean 50.68 / stdev 2.14, with 41/50 in 49-53; #40=45 and #46=58 give the claimed 13-point full-feed spread. lean_range (12-79) and lean_spread (mean 10.19) confirm member outlets genuinely span left-right while the cluster mean collapses to ~50. Root cause is substantially correct: the dominant, mathematically-guaranteed driver is cluster-mean cancellation of opposing leans across 30-50 balanced sources, and the length-adaptive blend at political_lean.py:887-895 plus the sigmoid dampening (x0=4, line 651) do push thin-RSS per-article scores toward center (the code's own comments at 869-876 admit this). Two imprecisions: (1) the cited line 632-634 returns 50.0 only in the zero-keyword branch, not a hard <4-keyword return; the >=4 is the sigmoid x0. (2) Thin text re-weights toward the SOURCE BASELINE (up to 0.50), so per-article scores retain lean signal rather than collapsing to 50 outright — though averaging opposed baselines still cancels to ~50, so the conclusion holds. Severity is overstated: "diversity indicator is cosmetic" is partly refuted because lean_spread/lean_range ARE computed and surfaced — HomeContent.tsx:465-475 maps them into biasSpread and Sigil.tsx:360/MicroSpectrum draws a bell whose width equals leanSpread. The genuine residual issue is that the DIRECTION indicator (BiasSnapshot leanLabel(politicalLean) and the spectrum center) reads ~Center for every cluster, hiding directional slant. That is an inherent property of a mean, not a regression or defect, and dispersion is already partly visualized. Valid observation, real evidence, but not high severity.

survivors.title: Single-source-dominated clusters presented as multi-source consensus; state media surfaced unlabeled
survivors.evidence: 8/50 clusters draw >=35% of their linked articles from one publisher: #24 'U.S. Dollar closes lower on Taipei forex market - Focus Taiwan' 32/50 = 64%, #38 'South Africa Cuts Fuel Prices...' 25/49 = 51%, #8 'Spain's Migrant Regularization' 23/50 = 46%, #33 'South Korea, Japan deepen AI and defense' 23/52 = 44%, #22 'North Carolina jail takeover' 16/37 = 43%, #28 'Santorini winemakers' 20/50 = 40%, #44 'Biden blasts Reflecting Pool' 19/49 = 39%, #46 'EU Sets October Deadline for China' 19/50 = 38%. #24 in particular is 64% wire-copy from a single outlet yet is presented with the same multi-source styling as diverse clusters. Separately, #23 '‘It could fall any moment’: RT reports from scene of Venezuela quake' is headlined by Russian state media (RT) with no state-affiliation label, and its consensus_points are the generic 'neutral framing' boilerplate.
survivors.root_cause: Neither feed_ranker nor the cluster export computes a source-concentration (single-publisher share / wire-duplication) penalty, so a 64%-one-outlet cluster ranks identically to a genuinely diverse one. is_wire_copy/wire_origin flags exist in the schema (per CLAUDE.md) but are not applied as a diversity discount here. State-media surfacing as a cluster headline is not penalized, and political_lean's state_affiliated correction (political_lean.py:867,897) does not propagate to a homepage label.
survivors.proposed_fix: Add a source-concentration term to rank_world that penalizes clusters where one publisher (or wire-origin group) exceeds ~35% of members, and surface a state-affiliated badge when the representative headline is from a state_affiliated source (RT #23). Also reconcile the source_count field with actual distinct source_id (mismatched on #12: 34 vs 40, #47: 40 vs 46) so the displayed diversity count is truthful.

verdict.reasoning: Every concrete claim verifies against scripts/top50_export.json. All 8 single-publisher concentration ratios match exactly (computed from _articles source_id counts): #24=32/50 (64%), #38=25/49 (51%), #8=23/50 (46%), #33=23/52 (44%), #22=16/37 (43%), #28=20/50 (40%), #44=19/49 (39%), #46=19/50 (38%); the "8/50 >=35%" count is precise (next are #13 34%, #11 33%, both below). #23 is titled "RT reports from scene of Venezuela quake" with generic boilerplate consensus_points, and RT is in data/sources.json as broadcast/far-right/RU. source_count mismatches confirmed: #12 34 vs 40 distinct, #47 40 vs 46. Root cause confirmed: pipeline/ranker/feed_ranker.py uses source_count only as a feed-lead minimum threshold (lines 154-183), with no single-publisher-share or wire-duplication penalty in the rank_world formula; no is_wire_copy/wire_origin/state_affiliated field exists in the export; political_lean.py:897 only floors the baseline scoring weight for state-affiliated sources and does not propagate a homepage label; frontend BiasSnapshot.tsx/HomeContent.tsx render bare source_count with no concentration adjustment, so #24 (64% one outlet) displays like a diverse cluster. Severity stays medium: real transparency/editorial-integrity gap (hidden concentration, unlabeled state media) but not a crash/security/feed-breaking defect. Only soft spot is the source_count-reconciliation sub-item, where the mismatch under-reports diversity rather than over-reports, but the cited values are accurate and it is a secondary fix.
